presentation: Headache in Pregnancy / Hypertensive Emergency of Pregnancy

sources.name: ACOG Practice Bulletin 222 - Gestational Hypertension and Preeclampsia

patient.communicationStyle: Cooperative and forthcoming. Her primary concern is the baby — she repeatedly asks 'Is my baby okay?' She's scared but tries to be composed. She knows something is wrong because her OB has been watching her blood pressure. She wants clear explanations and will ask questions.
patient.emotionalState: Frightened, focused on fetal wellbeing, cooperative
patient.occupation: Second-grade teacher, currently on maternity leave starting this week

responses.opening: I've had this terrible headache all day — it won't go away no matter what I do. And I'm seeing these spots... like flashing lights in my vision. My OB told me at my last appointment that my blood pressure was a little high and to watch for headaches, so I came right in. I'm 34 weeks pregnant. Is my baby okay?
responses.headache: It's been here all day, getting worse. It's like a pounding pressure all across my forehead and behind my eyes. Tylenol didn't touch it. It's probably a 7 out of 10. This isn't a normal headache for me.
responses.vision: I keep seeing these sparkly spots — like camera flashes or stars. They come and go. My vision gets a little blurry around the edges too. That's never happened before.
responses.swelling: My feet have been swollen for a couple weeks — my OB said that was normal for the third trimester. But the past two days my hands and face have gotten puffy too. My rings don't fit anymore. I woke up today and my face looked different.
responses.ruq: Now that you mention it... yes, actually, I've had this achy feeling right here (points to RUQ) since yesterday. I thought it was the baby kicking or maybe heartburn. It's more like a dull ache that doesn't go away.
responses.prenatalCourse: Everything was normal until about 3 weeks ago. My blood pressure started going up — my OB measured 140/88 and then 145/92 at my last visit. She said she was going to watch it closely and told me to come in if I got headaches, vision changes, or belly pain. She mentioned something called... preeclampsia?
responses.babyMovement: The baby's been moving, but maybe a little less today? I've been so focused on this headache I haven't done a kick count. That worries me.

patientPhrasing: terrible headache that won't go away
chiefComplaint.onset: Headache progressive over 12 hours. Visual symptoms began 6 hours ago. Known rising BP over past 3 weeks.
chiefComplaint.severity: Headache 7/10 unrelieved by acetaminophen. Visual symptoms intermittent but persistent.
chiefComplaint.gestationalAge: 34 weeks 2 days (confirmed by first-trimester dating ultrasound)

present.name: severe headache (unrelieved by acetaminophen)
present.patientSays: Pounding across my forehead and behind my eyes. Tylenol didn't help at all. This is a 7.

present.name: visual disturbances (scotomata, photopsia)
present.patientSays: Flashing lights, sparkly spots, blurry edges. Comes and goes but keeps happening.

present.name: right upper quadrant pain
present.patientSays: This dull ache under my ribs on the right side since yesterday.

present.name: rapid weight gain/facial edema
present.patientSays: My face is puffy and my rings don't fit. I gained 6 pounds in the last week.

present.name: hand and pedal edema (non-dependent)
present.patientSays: My hands are swollen — I can't make a full fist. Feet too.

present.name: nausea
present.patientSays: A little queasy since this morning.

present.name: decreased fetal movement (subjective)
present.patientSays: Maybe a little less than usual today.

absent.name: contractions
absent.patientSays: No contractions that I can tell.

absent.name: seizure
absent.patientSays: No, I haven't had a seizure.

absent.name: chest pain or dyspnea
absent.patientSays: No trouble breathing, no chest pain.

absent.name: epigastric burning (GERD-type)
absent.patientSays: This doesn't feel like heartburn. It's deeper, under my ribs.

prenatalHistory.prenatalCare: Regular — established care at 8 weeks, all visits attended
prenatalHistory.GBS: Pending (not yet screened — done at 36 weeks)
prenatalHistory.anatomy_scan: Normal 20-week anatomy scan. No fetal anomalies.
prenatalHistory.bpTrend: Normal until 31 weeks. 31wk: 138/84. 32wk: 140/88. 33wk: 145/92. OB documented gestational hypertension, counseled on warning signs.
prenatalHistory.urineProtein: Trace protein at 32 weeks (dipstick). Today: see diagnostics.

medications.name: Iron supplement
medications.dose: For mild anemia in second trimester (resolved)

socialHistory.occupation: Second-grade teacher, just started maternity leave
socialHistory.livingSituation: Lives with husband. Supportive. He's in the waiting room.
socialHistory.support: Good — husband, mother nearby, has OB she trusts

familyHistory: Mother: preeclampsia with patient's birth (delivered at 36 weeks)
No family history of seizures, clotting disorders, or chronic hypertension

present: rising BP trend over past 3 weeks

vitalSigns.fetalHeartRate: Category II tracing — baseline 155, moderate variability, intermittent variable decelerations with recovery (requires continued monitoring)

physicalExam.general: Anxious young woman, visibly edematous face and hands. No acute distress but uncomfortable. Oriented and appropriate.
physicalExam.heent: Facial edema (periorbital puffiness, cheek fullness — non-dependent edema). Fundoscopic exam: mild arteriolar narrowing, no papilledema, no hemorrhages.
physicalExam.cardiovascular: Tachycardic (92), regular. Loud S2 (hypertension). No murmurs.
physicalExam.pulmonary: Clear bilaterally. No crackles (no pulmonary edema currently).

abdominal.obstetric: Gravid uterus, fundal height 33cm (appropriate). Vertex presentation by Leopold maneuvers. Fetal heart tones audible.
abdominal.ruq: RIGHT UPPER QUADRANT TENDERNESS to palpation — hepatic capsule distension from HELLP. No rebound or guarding.

physicalExam.extremities: 3+ pitting edema bilateral lower extremities. Non-pitting edema of hands. Deep tendon reflexes: 3+ patellar bilaterally with 2-3 beats of CLONUS at ankles (hyperreflexia — CNS irritability, seizure risk indicator).
physicalExam.neurologic: Alert, oriented x4. Cranial nerves intact. Hyperreflexia with clonus (see extremities). No focal deficits.

labs.note: Significant proteinuria. Can also use 24-hour urine (>=300mg).
Supportive but spot ratio is more reliable
THROMBOCYTOPENIA (Plt <100) — meets severe feature criteria. Concerning for HELLP.
ELEVATED — hepatic involvement. AST >2x ULN = severe feature.
Hemolysis marker. Contributes to HELLP diagnosis.
Elevated for pregnancy. Renal involvement = severe feature if Cr >1.1.
MICROANGIOPATHIC HEMOLYTIC ANEMIA — confirms hemolysis component of HELLP
Must have — prepare for potential emergent delivery or hemorrhage

imaging.findings: Baseline FHR 155bpm. Moderate variability. Intermittent variable decelerations with recovery. Two accelerations in 20 minutes.
imaging.impression: Category II tracing — not reassuring but not ominous. Requires continued monitoring.

imaging.findings: Singleton viable fetus, vertex presentation. Estimated fetal weight 2,100g (10th percentile for 34 weeks — mild IUGR). AFI 6.2cm (borderline oligohydramnios). Placenta posterior, no abruption.
imaging.impression: Fetal growth restriction and borderline oligohydramnios — uteroplacental insufficiency from preeclampsia.

severeFeaturesCriteria.description: Preeclampsia with severe features requires hypertension + proteinuria PLUS any severe feature:
severeFeaturesCriteria.hellpAssessment: Meets criteria for PARTIAL HELLP: Hemolysis (schistocytes, low haptoglobin, elevated LDH) + Elevated Liver enzymes + Low Platelets.

immediateActions: Call OB attending STAT — delivery evaluation needed
Magnesium sulfate: 4-6g IV loading dose over 20-30 min, then 1-2g/hr (SEIZURE PROPHYLAXIS). Continue 24-48h postpartum.
Antihypertensive: IV labetalol 20mg, then 40mg, then 80mg every 10-20 min. OR IV hydralazine 5-10mg q20min. OR oral nifedipine 10mg immediate release q20min x3. TARGET: <160/110.
Foley catheter — strict I&O (monitor for oliguria)
Labs q6h: CBC, CMP, LDH, coags (trend for HELLP/DIC)
Anesthesia notification (epidural preferred if Plt allows; if Plt <70 → general anesthesia)
NICU notification (34 weeks — premature but viable)
Betamethasone 12mg IM x1 NOW, repeat in 24h (fetal lung maturity)

deliveryDecision.indication: Preeclampsia with severe features + HELLP at 34 weeks — DELIVERY IS INDICATED. Delivery is the ONLY definitive treatment.
deliveryDecision.timing: At >=34 weeks with severe features: do NOT delay. Deliver after maternal stabilization.
deliveryDecision.method: Induction of labor preferred if cervix favorable and fetal status reassuring. C-section for standard obstetric indications.

monitorFor: Loss of DTRs (first toxicity sign, ~7-10 mEq/L) — CHECK PATELLAR REFLEXES q1-2h
magnesiumMonitoring.antidote: Calcium gluconate 1g IV over 3 min — ALWAYS AT BEDSIDE

keyLearningPoints: Preeclampsia = new-onset hypertension (BP >=140/90) + proteinuria (or end-organ dysfunction) after 20 weeks gestation
Severe features include: BP >=160/110, Plt <100, transaminases >2x ULN, Cr >1.1, cerebral/visual symptoms, pulmonary edema, RUQ pain. Any ONE = severe.
HELLP (Hemolysis, Elevated Liver, Low Platelets) is a variant of severe preeclampsia. Check smear for schistocytes.
Magnesium sulfate is for SEIZURE PROPHYLAXIS, not BP control (MAGPIE trial).
Antihypertensive goal: <160/110. First-line: labetalol, hydralazine, nifedipine. NEVER use ACE inhibitors or ARBs in pregnancy.
DELIVERY is the only definitive treatment. At >=34 weeks with severe features: deliver after stabilization.
Betamethasone for fetal lung maturity at 24-36+6 weeks when delivery anticipated within 7 days.
Monitor Mg toxicity via DTRs q1-2h and urine output. Calcium gluconate is the antidote.
Hyperreflexia with clonus = CNS irritability = warning sign for eclamptic seizure.
mustNotMiss: HELLP syndrome (can progress to DIC, hepatic rupture)
Eclamptic seizure (can occur before, during, or AFTER delivery)
Pulmonary edema (capillary leak — be cautious with IV fluids)
commonPitfalls: Treating preeclampsia as 'just high blood pressure' — it's multisystem endothelial dysfunction
Using magnesium for BP control — it's for seizure prevention
Forgetting to check DTRs on magnesium — loss of reflexes precedes respiratory depression
Not having calcium gluconate at bedside
Delaying delivery at >=34 weeks to 'give steroids more time'
Assuming preeclampsia resolves at delivery — can WORSEN postpartum. Eclampsia can occur up to 6 weeks out.
Not checking peripheral smear — cannot diagnose HELLP hemolysis without it
Using ACE inhibitors or ARBs (teratogenic — renal agenesis, oligohydramnios)

expertDifferential.why: New hypertension + proteinuria + multiple severe features
Thrombocytopenia + schistocytes overlap. But TTP has more severe plt drop, normal LFTs, fever. Check ADAMTS13.
Similar presentation. But AFLP has hypoglycemia, hypofibrinogenemia.

teachingHooks.ifDoesntCheckPlatelets: You've identified the hypertension. But preeclampsia is more than BP — what labs define severity?
teachingHooks.ifUsesACEInhibitor: Red flag — what antihypertensives are absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy?
teachingHooks.ifForgetsCalciumGluconate: You've started magnesium. What's at the bedside in case of toxicity?
teachingHooks.ifWantsToDelayDelivery: She's 34 weeks with severe features and HELLP. What are the maternal risks of delaying?
teachingHooks.ifMissesHELLP: Platelets 98, AST 156, schistocytes on smear. What syndrome?
teachingHooks.ifAsksAboutPostpartum: When does preeclampsia go away? (Can worsen postpartum. Continue Mg 24-48h. Eclampsia up to 6 weeks out.)

patient.communicationStyle: Patient is POSTICTAL. All history from partner and EMS. After magnesium and stabilization, becomes progressively alert over 30-60 min but confused and frightened.
patient.emotionalState: Postictal confusion then frightened when alert

responses.partnerReport: (Visibly shaken) She was complaining about a headache all evening. Then said her vision was blurry. I told her we should go to the hospital and she was getting dressed when she dropped to the floor and started shaking. Her whole body was convulsing — eyes rolled back, stiff then shaking, maybe a minute or two. I called 911. She's 35 weeks pregnant. She's never had a seizure.
responses.emsReport: Postictal on scene. GCS 10. Witnessed tonic-clonic ~90 seconds. No recurrence in transport. BP in field 188/118. FHR present. No vaginal bleeding. No fall trauma. IV established, left lateral, O2 NRB.
responses.patientWhenAlert: (After 30 min, groggy) What happened? Where am I? (Reaches for belly) Is the baby okay? My head is killing me...

chiefComplaint.onset: Witnessed tonic-clonic seizure at home. ~90 seconds. No prior seizure history. 35 weeks pregnant.
chiefComplaint.collateralHistory: Partner: headache and visual blurring preceded seizure by several hours. No seizure disorder. No drugs.

prenatalHistory.prenatalCare: Inconsistent — missed last 2 appointments. BP not checked in 3 weeks.
prenatalHistory.lastKnownBP: 136/84 at 32 weeks (mildly elevated, not followed up)
prenatalHistory.notePrenatalGap: Missed visits = missed opportunity to diagnose worsening preeclampsia. Preventable outcome.

present: mildly elevated BP at last visit (not followed up)

vitalSigns.fetalHeartRate: Category III: baseline 170, minimal variability, repetitive late decelerations

physicalExam.general: Postictal. GCS 11. Left lateral. Tongue laceration. Diaphoretic. Confused.
physicalExam.neurologic: Postictal: confused, inconsistent command-following. Pupils 4mm equal reactive. Moving all extremities. Hyperreflexia 4+ with sustained clonus bilaterally.
physicalExam.abdominal: Gravid, firm. No vaginal bleeding. No abruption signs.

labs.note: Severe thrombocytopenia — HELLP. Regional anesthesia likely contraindicated.

immediateActions: Airway: suction, jaw thrust. Do NOT insert anything during active seizure.
Magnesium sulfate 6g IV over 15-20 min, then 2g/hr (FIRST-LINE — NOT phenytoin, NOT benzos)
If seizure recurs: additional 2g Mg bolus. If refractory: lorazepam 4mg IV or intubation.
Continuous fetal monitoring — Category III requires emergent delivery preparation.
OB, anesthesia, NICU at bedside STAT
EMERGENT C-SECTION under general anesthesia (Plt too low for regional, patient altered). Allow 15-20 min in utero resuscitation if tracing stabilizes; if not → immediate delivery.

keyLearningPoints: Eclampsia = new-onset tonic-clonic seizure in preeclampsia. Clinical diagnosis.
Magnesium sulfate is FIRST-LINE for eclamptic seizures — superior to phenytoin and diazepam (Eclampsia Trial Collaborative Group).
Do NOT give phenytoin for eclamptic seizures.
Left lateral decubitus: relieves aortocaval compression in pregnant patients.
Category III fetal tracing in eclampsia: prepare for emergent delivery, but allow brief in utero resuscitation (15-20 min) to stabilize mother.
Missed prenatal visits directly contributed — preeclampsia could have been caught earlier.
Eclampsia can occur antepartum, intrapartum, or POSTPARTUM (up to 6 weeks).
With Plt 72 and dropping fibrinogen: regional anesthesia contraindicated → general anesthesia.
teaching.attendingQuestions: EMS brings a 35-week pregnant woman who seized at home. Postictal, BP 188/118, Category III tracing. You have 20 minutes. Walk me through exactly what you're doing and why. Why magnesium and not phenytoin? When do you cut?
